humanities_role_explanation: The benchmark uses humanities subjects (e.g., History, Philosophy, World Religions) as primary domains to investigate cultural bias, while also analyzing the philological and linguistic aspects of translation (translationese vs. human translation) across 42 languages.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: A mono-modal text benchmark featuring multiple-choice questions and answers translated or post-edited across multiple languages.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Evaluates factual recognition and contextual reasoning in specific humanities and social science disciplines. By categorizing questions into Culturally-Sensitive (requiring localized historical, geographic, or cultural frames) and Culturally-Agnostic subsets, it assesses whether models can reason within non-Western epistemic frameworks.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Global-MMLU is a multilingual and culturally-annotated adaptation of MMLU, a standard general-purpose LLM benchmark covering 57 subject areas.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates 14 open-weight and proprietary models, including Aya Expanse, Command R/R+, Gemma 2, Llama 3.1, Mistral Nemo, Qwen 2.5, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The LLMs are the primary systems being benchmarked for their multilingual and cross-cultural knowledge capabilities.

arts_humanities_data.note: Includes substantial humanities data in ASJC areas: History (US History, European History, World History), Philosophy, Religious Studies (World Religions), and Language and Linguistics (translation quality and dialect analysis).

quantitative_metrics.note: Utilizes accuracy (%), model rank changes, and position shifts to measure performance.

published_after_march_2023.note: Released as an arXiv preprint on February 20, 2025.

english_language.note: The paper is written in English, and the benchmark evaluates English alongside 41 other languages.

summary: This paper presents Global-MMLU, an improved multilingual and culturally annotated version of the MMLU dataset spanning 42 languages. The authors audit the original English MMLU for cultural biases, showing that the majority of culturally sensitive questions are heavily skewed toward Western-centric concepts. By splitting the benchmark into Culturally-Sensitive and Culturally-Agnostic subsets, the paper provides a more nuanced framework for evaluating how generative models perform when stripped of Western cultural baselines.
key_findings: The authors find that 28% of MMLU questions require culturally sensitive knowledge, with 86.5% of those being Western-specific and 84.9% of geographic questions focusing on North America or Europe. Evaluating 14 state-of-the-art models reveals that their rankings are highly volatile when evaluated on Culturally-Sensitive versus Culturally-Agnostic subsets (showing an average of 5.7 rank changes for the CS subset compared to 3.4 for the CA subset), particularly for smaller models and lower-resource languages.
